Title: No Company Has Admitted to Replacing Workers With AI in New York
URL: https://www.wired.com/story/no-company-has-admitted-to-replacing-workers-with-ai-in-new-york/
Time Published: 2026-02-09T12:00:00Z
Full Content:
Over 160 companies in New York state have filed notices of mass layoffs since last March. None—in a group that includes Amazon, Goldman Sachs, and other employers that are adopting AI tools—attributed their workforce cuts in those filings to “technological innovation or automation.” That option was added 11 months ago to a required question on paperwork that businesses with 50 or more employees must file with the state to notify of sizable job losses. New York’s Department of Labor told WIRED that, as of the end of January, no employer had marked tech as the reason for their workforce reduction. Over the past couple of years, many companies have celebrated offloading repetitive tasks such as customer service, sales, and accounting to AI systems. But saying that they are dumping human workers in favor of AI agents or robots can risk reputational harm. And economists face challenges tracing layoffs to tech advancements because companies can take decades to fully reorganize around new ways of working. Enter New York governor Kathy Hochul. To get a better handle on the current reality, she ordered the Department of Labor to begin asking whether AI had been the impetus for layoffs. New York became the first state with an AI option, according to legal experts. New York businesses can select multiple reasons among a list of 17 in total that also includes “bankruptcy,” “merger,” “relocation,” and “other,” on required Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification, or WARN, filings. If any company were to select the tech and automation option, they would receive an additional question asking them to specify the technology taking over work, such as AI, robotics, or “software modernization.” Over 750 notices spanning 162 employers and affecting nearly 28,300 workers have followed the rollout without AI coming up. The results suggest that companies may be dodging the AI question. Or it’s a sign that workers need not yet fear anything more than the traditional drivers of layoffs. Some of the filers include caterers and retailers whose staff haven’t widely been linked to capable AI replacements. On the other hand, Goldman Sachs led the way with more than 4,100 workers affected by layoffs or location closures, according to New York records. Amazon was among the top 10 with 660 affected workers. Morgan Stanley, another AI adopter, reported 260 workers out of job. Internally, Goldman Sachs linked its layoffs last year to AI’s potential to unlock significant productivity gains. Amazon warned ahead of its latest waves of layoffs, which affected about 30,000 workers in total, that benefits from AI would lead to job cuts. An unnamed source told Bloomberg that a small portion of Morgan Stanley’s layoffs reflected AI and automation use. The companies operate around the world, so it’s possible that only employees outside of New York were pushed out in favor of AI. Overall, nearly 55,000 US companies attributed job cuts to adoption of AI last year, according to an analysis of public statements by the job search firm Challenger, Gray & Christmas. Still, none of these developments showing up in the unique New York data reinforces the challenge with answering the question on everyone’s mind: “Is AI going to take my job?” Amazon spokesperson Kelly Nantel says, “AI is not the reason behind the vast majority” of cuts and that instead the goal is “reducing layers, increasing ownership, and helping reduce bureaucracy.” Goldman Sachs declined to comment. Morgan Stanley didn’t respond to requests for comment. WARN filings are intended to give state agencies advance notice of cuts, so that they can ramp up services to help people quickly find new jobs. Companies face $500 daily fines for noncompliance with filing requirements. Kristin Devoe, a spokesperson for the governor, says the Department of Labor follows up with every employer to ensure the accuracy of filings. In the case of Amazon, for example, the company listed “economic” as the rationale for layoffs, according to Devoe. It explained to the department that employees hired during the pandemic to meet surges in online shopping were no longer needed. “The data collected through WARN notices, including the reason for layoff, serves as a valuable indicator of shifts in the labor market,” Devoe says. “Gathering these early indicators allows the state to identify workforce trends and upskill workers who may need retraining to make their skills more compatible with today’s changing economy.” She added that it is crucial for employers to answer “questions in WARN frankly and honestly so New York State can support displaced workers to the greatest extent possible.” One of the country’s largest union groups, the New York State AFL-CIO, applauds Hochul for trying to capture data on AI-related layoffs. It suggests that additional rules and greater focus on the issue could help bring about meaningful information. “We must establish specific regulations that mandate employer accountability and transparency in AI deployment and ensure employers comply,” says Mario Cilento, the federation’s president. Last month, state lawmaker Harry Bronson, who chairs the Assembly Labor Committee, introduced two bills that could expand reporting of AI-fueled job loss. One would require businesses with more than 100 employees to file annual estimates with the state on the number of unfilled roles as a result of increasing use of AI, and how many employees worked more or less hours because of AI. The other bill would resemble WARN procedures but apply to a wider set of businesses handing over jobs to computers. Notably, failure to report could impair a company’s ability to get state grants and tax breaks—a new prod to gather data. Erica Groshen, a labor economist at Cornell University, which is in New York, says employers will struggle with answering questions about the consequences of new tech. A better requirement, she says, would be for companies to share detailed data with labor agencies on the evolution of skills and occupations. “Frankly, do we really care if someone is displaced by AI, or just the normal competitive marketplace?” Groshen asks. What lawmakers ought to do is “give people the information they need to make the right transitions.”
--------------------------------------------------

Title: What parents need to know about Trump Accounts: An FAQ | CNN Business
URL: https://www.cnn.com/2026/02/09/business/parents-trump-accounts-kids-faq
Time Published: 2026-02-09T11:50:18Z
Full Content:
Markets Hot Stocks Fear & Greed Index Latest Market News Hot Stocks There has been a lot of attention paid to the new Trump Accounts – especially the promised $1,000 federal government contributions for eligible newborns and the public declarations by companies and philanthropists that they will be making contributions, too. But the fine print on exactly how the accounts will work is still emerging and there is a lot of information and press releases to sort through from the Treasury, the IRS, the White House and the official site trumpaccounts.gov. So … we’ve tried to create a one-stop shop with the most immediately salient points for parents interested in creating an account for their child. Here is what we know so far (and where we will make updates as Treasury and the IRS provide more details and clarity). Trump Accounts are IRA-style savings accounts for eligible children. They are like traditional IRAs in that money in the accounts will grow tax deferred. But the rules for Trump Accounts differ when it comes to contributions, withdrawals and approved uses of the money. Also, the money may not be tapped before a child turns 18. A child must be under age 18 at the end of the year in which an account is opened for them, per the IRS. The child also must be a U.S. citizen and have a valid Social Security number. A child is only allowed to have one Trump account opened for them. And it must be opened by an “authorized individual” – which is generally a legal guardian or parent. (The IRS breaks out more details on that here.) Any child eligible to have a Trump Account and who is born between January 1, 2025 and December 31, 2028 may receive a one-time, $1,000 pilot contribution from the federal government. But only if an authorized individual has opened an account for them and may claim them as a dependent on their tax return, according to enrolled agent David Mellem of Ashwaubenon Tax Professionals. Parents must elect to open an account for their child by filling out and submitting Form 4547. This is the same form on which you will elect to get the $1,000 pilot contribution for your eligible babies. At the moment, the best way to do that is to submit the form electronically with your 2025 federal income tax return. But, starting this summer, according to trumpaccounts.gov, there will be an online portal for all parents to establish an account. Based on the information requested on Form 4547 it appears parents are required to have a Social Security number. But IRS instructions for the form say that if the parent is “a nonresident or resident alien and you don’t have and aren’t eligible to get an SSN, enter your IRS individual taxpayer identification number.”) Once a parent submits Form 4547, starting in May 2026 “the Treasury Department or its agent will send information to the individual who made the election to activate the account through an authentication process and complete the opening of the initial Trump Account,” the IRS said in preliminary guidance. Per the IRS, “no pilot program contribution will be deposited in the Trump Account of a child earlier than July 4, 2026.” But more generally, it said, Treasury “will make the pilot program contribution as soon as practicable after the election is made and the Treasury Department can confirm with the initial Trump account trustee that the initial Trump account has been opened.” Beyond the federal government’s one-time contribution, several parties may contribute to a child’s account. But the rules and limitations differ for each group. Employers: They may make deductible contributions to the account of an employee’s child. That money will be tax-free to the employee. The employer’s contribution may not exceed $2,500 a year per employee, not per child, Mellem said. That annual limit will be adjusted for cost of living after 2027, per initial guidance from the IRS. To date, several employers – such as JPMorgan Chase and BlackRock – have said they intend to offer their employees a match to the federal government’s seed contribution to Trump Accounts. Family and friends: Parents, grandparents and other individuals may make contributions too. But they will not get a deduction for their contributions. States, qualified nonprofit organizations and philanthropists: Their contributions must be made to “members of a qualified class” as the IRS puts it – that could mean, for example, all children born in a specific year or all children living in a given state. Some business leaders – notably Michael Dell – have pledged to make one-time $250 seed contributions to the accounts of certain groups of children from middle- to lower-income households. Family and employer contributions combined may not exceed $5,000 a year for a single account. That limit will be adjusted for cost of living starting in 2027. Contributions from governments and nonprofits will not count toward the limit, per the IRS guidance. The money must be invested in a low-cost, broadly diversified US stock index fund or exchange-traded fund. Low cost is defined as an expense ratio of no more than 0.10% – so for every $1,000 in an account the annual fee can’t exceed $1 a year. What’s not clear yet: Which funds will be approved for use by the federal government. Initially, they will “be created and held with Treasury’s designated financial agent,” according to a White House document. An agent is a financial institution of Treasury’s choosing. After that initial period, the White House noted, “parents or guardians will be able to transfer the full balance of a Trump Account to their preferred brokerage firm through a simple trustee-to-trustee rollover.” The key to growing a sizable sum depends on how much is contributed to the account every year and how well the index or exchange-traded fund in a child’s account performs. This table breaks out some examples. The money generally may not be touched until the child turns 18. At that point, it may be withdrawn or it can be left invested. When money is taken out, most of it will be subject to income tax (with the exception of the portion that comes from the nondeductible contributions made by, say, a parent, according to Mellem.) In addition, depending on how it’s used, the money may be subject to a 10% penalty. The child may use the money without penalty to help pay for college or buy a first home. But if the money is taken out before age 59-1/2 and is used for non-approved expenses – e.g., emergencies, credit card debt payments, etc. – it will be subject to a 10% penalty on top of the ordinary income tax owed. (It’s worth noting that promoters of the accounts often assert a child may also use the money as an adult to help start a business, but it is not clear from existing materials that such use would be exempt from the 10% penalty.) Making an investment in children’s future from birth is a welcome idea in many quarters. And in the best circumstances – a child’s family can afford to contribute money every year, the parent works for an employer offering an additional contribution, the stock market does well during the “growth period” of 18 years, etc. – a Trump Account may provide a much-needed source of funds for today’s children to help offset the costs of college and early adulthood. But many families won’t be able to afford to make many – or any – contributions. One critique of the accounts is that they will disproportionately benefit families who already have means. Madeline Brown, a senior policy associate at the Urban Institute, questions the utility of Trump accounts after the $1,000 pilot ends for lower-income households, since they already have low participation rates when it comes to saving for their children’s future in other tax-advantaged plans like 529s or Roth IRAs. “About a third of families don’t have $2,000 in emergency savings, so it’s no surprise that they don’t have the means to go start saving in [other plans] for their children,” Brown said. CNN’s Julian Torres contributed to this report. Most stock quote data provided by BATS. US market indices are shown in real time, except for the S&P 500 which is refreshed every two minutes. Title: From watchdogs to mouthpieces: Washington Post and the wreckage of legacy media
URL: https://www.thejournal.ie/readme/bezos-washington-post-trump-6950317-Feb2026/
Time Published: 2026-02-09T11:32:45Z
Full Content:
We need your help now Support from readers like you keeps The Journal open. You are visiting us because we have something you value. Independent, unbiased news that tells the truth. Advertising revenue goes some way to support our mission, but this year it has not been enough. If you've seen value in our reporting, please contribute what you can, so we can continue to produce accurate and meaningful journalism. For everyone who needs it. Sign in. It’s quick, free and it’s up to you. An account is an optional way to support the work we do. Find out more. LAST UPDATE | 9 Feb HAVING JUST SUFFERED another 300 newsroom layoffs, the closure of sections and bureaus, the exodus of many of its most venerable journalists, and the “moral infirmity” — in the words of a former editor — of transforming its editorial page into a Trumpist mouthpiece, The Washington Post is a dead newspaper walking. It is far from alone in the graveyard. In the US, most newspaper chains are controlled by hedge funds milking them dry, or by billionaires — The Post’s Jeff Bezos or the Los Angeles Times’ Patrick Soon-Shiong — harpooning their souls. Amazon billionaire Jeff Bezos (l) owns The Washington Post, while Dr. Patrick Soon-Shiong owns the Los Angeles Times. Magazines, as a media genre, are a ghost of their former selves. In the US — unlike in Europe — broadcast radio has become irrelevant. Broadcast television’s audience is geriatric, and its owners are gutless media conglomerates. Disney knuckled under to Trump and pulled Jimmy Kimmel off ABC. CBS is now in the clutches of mini Murdochs named Ellison and their agent of destruction, contrarianist Bari Weiss, who has turned the networks’ once revered nightly news into state media. On shrinking cable, Fox News is the Murdochs’ political organ, MSNOW (née MSNBC) has been sloughed off onto a funereal ice floe by Comcast-NBC, and CNN is at risk of also falling under the Ellisons’ thumb. Larry Ellison, left, daughter Megan Ellison and David Ellison, right. Alamy Stock Photo Alamy Stock Photo Ah, but you say, at least America still has The New York Times. Yes, and there are good journalists doing good work there. But on the whole, The Times has failed to protect American democracy. Advertisement The paper constantly normalises the rabid insanities of the current administration as policy. It both-sides extremism, not merely opening the Overton window but smashing it. Worst of all, The Times refuses to name and explain fascism. It says we are on the road to autocracy. Oh, we have arrived. Facebook CEO Mark Zuckerberg (L), Lauren Sanchez, wife of Jeff Bezos, (right) at Trump's inauguration. Alamy Stock Photo Alamy Stock Photo Though there are exceptions — independently owned newspapers and public media upholding journalistic values — it must be said that, on the whole, legacy, mass media is dead or dying. I say this with regret, for I have devoted my life and career to journalism and media at scale. And I have spent countless hours on journalism conference panels hypothesising new business models to save what was. Now I see that instead, we need to build what can be. In my research on media history for my books The Gutenberg Parenthesis, Magazine, and Hot Type, one clear lesson emerged: that nothing in media or news is forever. The newspaper was invented a century and a half after Gutenberg (at about the same time as the modern novel and the essay). It was reinvented with the introduction of steam-powered presses, the Linotype typesetting machine, and the telegraph. Jarvis' new book, Hot Type, The Magnificent Machine that Gave Birth to Mass Media and Drove Mark Twain Mad, is out now. With the mechanisation and industrialisation of publishing, media became the province of capital and corporations, which seek scale über alles. In the late nineteenth century, a new business model emerged: selling audiences to advertisers. It was a lovely and profitable oligopoly for the privileged few proprietors from the 1890s to the 1990s — until the internet co-opted and killed mass media’s attention economy. What will replace the media that was? It is too soon to tell. In the late 19th century, it was unclear which media might persevere, in what form, performing what cultural functions. Sunday newspapers published magazines. Magazines published books. Book publishers founded magazines. Today, it’s an open question which forms might emerge victorious: newspapers, magazines, and TV (if any survive) against blogs, newsletters, podcasts and social media. And then there’s AI, the literate machine, joining in public discourse. Related Reads From white knight to grim reaper: Bezos guts the Washington Post Larry Donnelly: Despite continued criticism, it's full speed ahead for the Trump administration Larry Donnelly: From Washington to the Áras, uncertainty reigns on both sides of the Atlantic But it’s not the form that will matter; it is, of course, the substance. There will still be demand for accountability, investigation and explanation — for journalism. But journalism can and must change to meet new needs and opportunities. Dustin Hoffman & Robert Redford in All the President's Men, 1976, the story of the Watergate scandal uncovered by journalists Bob Woodward and Carl Bernstein at The Washington Post. Alamy Stock Photo Alamy Stock Photo Look to Minneapolis, where witnesses perform critical acts of journalism. News must be defined more collaboratively. And I welcome the day when media return to human scale, no longer claiming to serve everyone the same, no longer requiring capital at scale to start and survive. I do regret the passing of zombie The Washington Post, mostly because for a brief, impressive moment under the early ownership of Jeff Bezos and the editorship of the now-retired Marty Baron, it presented welcome competition to The New York Times, driving each to be better. Now, its reputation is ruined, its talent gone, its audience deserting, its business in shambles. “Democracy dies in darkness” was its slogan. After chopping jobs and whole departments, Bezos’ hired henchman, publisher Will Lewis, announced he was leaving. He should turn the lights off on the way out. To owner Bezos, the Post is a useful prop to please Trump for the sake of larger business interests. To democracy, it is becoming all but useless. I hear no talk of anyone else rescuing The Post from its supposed saviour. The time has come instead to replace it. Jeff Jarvis is a US journalist, author and editor. He is a journalism professor in New York and author of the upcoming book Hot Type: The Magnificent Machine that Gave Birth to Mass Media and Drove Mark Twain Mad. Find him at @jeffjarvis. Title: Arthur Hayes Reveals Key Reason For Bitcoin’s Decline As Banks And BlackRock Come Under Fire
URL: https://zycrypto.com/arthur-hayes-reveals-key-reason-for-bitcoins-decline-as-banks-and-blackrock-come-under-fire/
Time Published: 2026-02-09T10:31:50Z
Full Content:
BitMEX co-founder Arthur Hayes has pointed fingers at large US banks for Bitcoin’s recent price slump. Bitcoin (BTC) price slumped below $65,000 in a double-digit percentage drawdown over the last seven days. According to an X post, Hayes theorized that Bitcoin’s decline is intricately linked to hedging by US financial institutions on BlackRock’s IBIT structured products. He noted that the recent price drop was not triggered by organic buying and selling of BTC, pegging the slump to dealer hedging.At the heart of Hayes’ claim are structured products issued by major banks and tied to BlackRock’s Bitcoin ETF. Typically, when a bank sells a structured note, they manage risk by buying or selling the underlying asset to offset its exposure. Hayes argues that a specific price level for the product triggered banks to sell large amounts of Bitcoin, sparking the weeklong price decline. The BitMEX co-founder disclosed plans to publish a list of Morgan Stanley’s issued notes to identify the trigger points for mass BTC sales. “BTC dump probably due to dealer hedging off the back of IBIT structured products,” said Hayes. “I will be compiling a complete list of all issued notes by the bank to better understand trigger points that could cause rapid price rises and falls.” However, his decision raised eyebrows with one pundit questioning its importance “after the move had already happened.” In his defence, Hayes noted that if his research flags an interesting trigger point, he will take a position against it and publish his findings. “When we flag an interesting trigger point, we will take positions against it and tell you guys about it after,” said Hayes According to CoinMarketCap data, the BTC price fell below its 2021 all-time high of $69,000, reaching a multi-year low of $60,074. The asset is down 15% on the seven-day charts, with a near-50% decline from peak levels. Meanwhile, the rest of the market has recorded a jarring decline in the wake of BTC’s slump. Ethereum (ETH) is barely holding above $2,000, while XRP and BNB have shed over 14% of their market value in under a week. At press time, the global crypto market capitalization stands at $2.35 trillion, with 24-hour trading volume down 40%.

Title: Top 10 Shopify Products for Designers That Actually Make Money in 2026
URL: https://weandthecolor.com/top-10-shopify-products-for-designers-that-actually-make-money-in-2026/208110
Time Published: 2026-02-09T09:53:05Z
Full Content:
This post contains affiliate links. We may earn a commission if you click on them and make a purchase. It’s at no extra cost to you and helps us run this site. Thanks for your support! Shopify products for designers have evolved beyond aesthetic experiments. The platform now rewards creators who solve real problems with high-margin digital goods. Meanwhile, traditional print-on-demand t-shirts barely break even anymore. Consequently, smart designers are pivoting toward what I call “Solution Commerce”—selling tools that eliminate friction in other people’s workflows. The numbers tell a clear story. Digital templates now command 95% profit margins after Shopify fees. Furthermore, educational products build authority while generating passive income streams. In contrast, physical inventory ties up capital and creates fulfillment headaches. Therefore, this shift toward digital-first selling isn’t just trendy—it’s financially rational. You’re competing with thousands of designers on Shopify. However, most of them are selling undifferentiated products in oversaturated niches. Additionally, they lack the strategic frameworks that separate hobbyists from six-figure sellers. This article introduces the “Designer Revenue Stack”—a methodology for building multiple income streams through complementary product types. I’ve spent years analyzing successful design-focused Shopify stores. Moreover, I’ve consulted with creators earning $10K to $50K monthly through digital products alone. Their success follows predictable patterns. Specifically, they target the creator economy’s infrastructure needs rather than consumer trends. Ultimately, this approach creates sustainable businesses instead of flash-in-the-pan successes. The answer depends on your skill set and risk tolerance. Nevertheless, certain categories consistently outperform others in both margin and scalability. Let’s examine each through the lens of what I call “Profit Velocity”—the speed at which your initial effort converts to recurring revenue. Before exploring specific products, you need to understand how profitable designers structure their offerings. The Designer Revenue Stack consists of three tiers. First, Gateway Products attract customers at low price points ($5-$25). Second, Core Revenue Products deliver the bulk of income ($30-$150). Third, Authority Products establish expertise while commanding premium prices ($200-$5,000). Successful Shopify designers rarely sell just one product type. Instead, they create complementary offerings that move customers up the value ladder. For instance, someone buying Instagram templates might later purchase a complete brand kit. Eventually, that same customer invests in a design course teaching advanced techniques. Notion templates represent the perfect digital Shopify product for designers. They require zero production cost after initial creation. Additionally, they scale infinitely without quality degradation. Most importantly, Notion’s 2026 user base exceeds 100 million people actively seeking better organizational systems. The market has evolved beyond generic productivity templates. Instead, buyers now want “Second Brain” systems tailored to specific professions. For example, “Notion for Interior Designers” sells far better than “Notion Productivity Template.” Similarly, “Freelance Writer’s Content Hub” outperforms generic writing planners. Your profit margin hovers around 95% after Shopify transaction fees. Furthermore, customers often purchase multiple templates as their needs evolve. Therefore, you’re building a customer base that returns seasonally for new systems. UI/UX designers particularly excel at Notion template creation. Their skills in information architecture translate directly to workspace design. Moreover, they understand how to make complex databases feel intuitive and visually appealing. The 2026 trend focuses on integration-heavy templates. Buyers want workspaces that connect Notion with tools like Slack, Gmail, and Asana. Consequently, templates featuring advanced automation and API connections command premium prices ($75-$150). Social media brand kits solve a critical problem for small businesses. They need daily content but lack design resources. Therefore, “done-for-you” template bundles become essential business tools rather than nice-to-have extras. Your profit margin reaches approximately 95% on these digital products. Additionally, the pricing sweet spot ($20-$50) encourages impulse purchases. Most importantly, customers return every quarter for seasonal template refreshes. Single templates rarely convert well anymore. Instead, offer comprehensive packages like “100 Real Estate Instagram Posts + 50 Story Templates.” Specifically, buyers want enough variety to post for three months without repeating designs. Industry-specific bundles dramatically outperform generic offerings. For instance, “Wellness Coach Social Media Kit” targets a specific pain point. Similarly, “Restaurant Instagram Templates” speaks directly to cafe and bistro owners. Consequently, your conversion rates double compared to generic social media templates. Canva’s API integration allows you to sell templates that buyers can customize instantly. Moreover, this interactivity increases perceived value without additional work from you. Therefore, you’re competing on convenience rather than pure design skill. Selling design systems represents the premium tier of digital Shopify products for designers. You’re packaging your professional workflow for other creators. Furthermore, developers pay $100-$500 per license to avoid building interfaces from scratch. The market demands specificity here, too. Generic UI kits gather dust while “SaaS Dashboard Design System” or “E-commerce Mobile App Kit” sell consistently. Additionally, buyers want production-ready files that integrate with their existing tech stack. Your ideal customer is a developer or small agency racing against deadlines. They value time savings above everything else. Moreover, they’re willing to pay premium prices for thoroughly documented, well-organized files. Figma files dominate the market currently. However, React component libraries and Tailwind CSS kits are gaining traction. Consequently, consider your technical skills when choosing which format to offer. Nevertheless, documentation quality matters more than file format. The most successful design systems include real-world examples and use cases. For instance, showing the same button component across ten different states helps buyers implement it correctly. Similarly, providing dark mode alternatives doubles the system’s utility. Samsung’s Frame TV transformed digital art into functional home decor. Subsequently, similar products from LG and TCL expanded the market significantly. Therefore, high-resolution digital art now serves a practical purpose beyond screen backgrounds. The key differentiator is aspect ratio optimization. Frame TVs require specific dimensions that most stock photos don’t provide. Moreover, buyers want curated collections that match their interior design style. Consequently, you’re selling curation expertise alongside the artwork itself. Seasonal galleries encourage customers to refresh their displays regularly. For example, “The Winter Landscape Collection” sells in November and December. Then, “Spring Botanical Gallery” converts those same customers in March. Additionally, holiday-specific collections create urgency around limited-time offerings. Your profit margin reaches 100% on digital art files. Furthermore, Frame TV owners typically have higher disposable incomes than average consumers. Therefore, they’re comfortable paying $15-$40 for a curated gallery pack. The competition remains relatively low compared to generic printable art. Additionally, Frame TV communities actively seek new content creators. Moreover, organic word-of-mouth drives significant traffic once you establish quality standards. 3D printing files (STL/OBJ formats) represent what I call “The New POD.” You sell the design while customers handle production. Consequently, you eliminate shipping costs, inventory risk, and fulfillment headaches entirely. The market has specific high-demand niches. Custom mechanical keyboard keycaps appeal to enthusiasts willing to pay premium prices. Moreover, tabletop gaming miniatures serve a dedicated community that constantly needs new characters. Additionally, “IKEA hacks”—custom brackets, handles, and organizers—solve everyday problems creatively. Hobbyists with 3D printers actively seek new designs to test their machines. Furthermore, they appreciate the instant gratification of immediate downloads. Therefore, your conversion rates often exceed those of physical product stores. Profit margins hit 100% since fulfillment is completely digital. However, you must understand basic 3D modeling principles to create printable designs. Moreover, providing printing guides and support parameters increases buyer confidence significantly. The community aspect drives long-term success here. Joining Reddit’s r/3Dprinting or specialized Discord servers connects you with potential customers. Additionally, sharing your printing process builds trust and demonstrates expertise. A successful typeface generates income for years after initial creation. Commercial licensing fees range from $50 for small businesses to $5,000 for major brands. Moreover, fonts serve as passive income machines once they gain traction. The strategy requires patience and marketing sophistication. First, offer a “Personal Use” version at a low cost ($5-$15) to build exposure. Then, charge significantly more for “Commercial/Web” licenses ($50-$200). Consequently, your free/cheap tier drives awareness while commercial licenses generate profit. Trending aesthetics change yearly, creating opportunities for timely typefaces. For instance, the 2026 surge in “Neo-Brutalism” design created demand for bold, geometric fonts. Similarly, the “Quiet Luxury” trend increased interest in refined serif typefaces. Technical quality matters enormously in font design. Buyers expect proper kerning, complete character sets, and web font formats. Additionally, providing OpenType features like ligatures increases perceived value significantly. MyFonts and Creative Market dominate font distribution. However, selling directly through Shopify lets you keep more profit per sale. Moreover, you control pricing tiers and can offer exclusive licenses to premium clients. Standard t-shirt POD stores barely survive in 2026. However, aesthetic home goods command much higher margins and perceived value. Weighted blankets, woven wall tapestries, and designer area rugs create memorable products that customers showcase. Apps like Printify and Gelato sync seamlessly with Shopify. Consequently, you design once, and the platform handles production and shipping. Moreover, you test products without financial risk or inventory investment. Home decor purchases are less frequent but more considered. Buyers spend time finding the perfect piece for their space. Therefore, they’re willing to pay $60-$200 for items that match their aesthetic vision. Your design approach should emphasize cohesive collections rather than individual pieces. For instance, a “Desert Minimalism Collection” might include throw pillows, wall art, and blankets. Similarly, offering matching sets increases average order value significantly. Seasonal refreshes work exceptionally well in home decor. Customers redecorate for different seasons or life events. Consequently, targeting specific moments (new home, nursery setup, office redesign) improves conversion rates dramatically. Startups can’t afford $5,000 custom brand identities initially. However, they need professional-looking materials to launch credibly. Therefore, “semi-custom” branding templates fill a crucial market gap. The key is selling complete Brand Bible templates instead of just logos. Include typography guidelines, color palettes, and social media mockups. Additionally, provide usage examples across business cards, websites, and packaging. A logo alone leaves buyers uncertain about implementation. Conversely, a comprehensive brand guide empowers them to maintain consistency across touchpoints. Moreover, this completeness justifies higher prices ($75-$150). Your ideal customer is a solopreneur or small agency launching quickly. They value speed and affordability over uniqueness. Furthermore, they plan to invest in custom branding once they achieve product-market fit. Offering customization services as an upsell dramatically increases revenue. For instance, buyers purchase the template for $100, then pay $300 for personalization. Consequently, you’re building both passive and active income streams. The iPad-as-notebook trend continues dominating in 2026. Digital planners for GoodNotes and Notability convert exceptionally well on social media. Moreover, the seasonal nature of planners creates predictable revenue spikes. Everyone wants a fresh planner in January. Additionally, students purchase back-to-school planners in August. Therefore, timing your launches around these moments maximizes sales potential significantly. Hyperlinked PDFs with functional navigation separate great planners from mediocre ones. Buyers expect to tap dates and jump between sections seamlessly. Moreover, including editable text fields increases usability across devices. Niche planners outperform generic ones consistently. “Freelance Designer Project Planner” sells better than “Daily Planner.” Similarly, “Fitness Trainer Client Management Journal” targets a specific pain point effectively. The aesthetic matters enormously for digital planners. Your cover design determines click-through rates on Instagram and Pinterest. Furthermore, interior page layouts should balance functionality with visual appeal carefully. Your specific design skills represent your most valuable Shopify product. Packaging expertise as mini-courses or process ebooks generates both income and authority. Moreover, educational products make your other offerings more credible and valuable. Format matters here. “How to Master Grainy Gradients in Illustrator” works as a 30-page PDF with video examples. Similarly, “My Complete Logo Design Process” could be a video course with workbooks. Consequently, choose formats matching your teaching style and technical capabilities. Customers who learn from you trust your other products implicitly. Furthermore, courses position you as an expert rather than just another designer. Therefore, your templates, fonts, and other products gain perceived value automatically. Pricing should reflect the transformation you’re providing. A comprehensive course that saves students 100+ hours of trial-and-error deserves $200-$500. Moreover, this price point attracts serious learners who implement your teachings actively. Launch strategies matter enormously for educational products. Building email waitlists and creating pre-launch content drives initial sales momentum. Additionally, offering early-bird discounts rewards your most engaged followers appropriately. Starting a Shopify store feels overwhelming initially. However, the platform simplifies technical aspects significantly. Moreover, following a systematic setup process prevents costly mistakes later. The Basic Shopify plan ($39/month) suits most designers starting out. It includes everything needed for digital product sales. Furthermore, you can upgrade to the Grow plan ($105/month) once you’re processing 50+ orders monthly. Note that annual billing saves 25% on these prices. Avoid the Starter plan ($5/month) for digital products. It lacks essential features like abandoned cart recovery and a full storefront. Moreover, the Basic plan’s 2.9% + 30¢ transaction fees become economical quickly. Digital Downloads (free Shopify app) handles automatic file delivery securely. Customers receive download links immediately after purchase. Additionally, the app tracks download attempts and limits to prevent abuse. SendOwl or FetchApp provides more sophisticated digital delivery options. They include PDF stamping and license key generation. However, most designers succeed with Shopify’s built-in Digital Downloads app initially. Your homepage should showcase your best-selling product immediately. Furthermore, high-quality mockups demonstrating product usage increase trust dramatically. Avoid cluttered layouts that confuse visitors about your offerings. Product pages require detailed descriptions explaining exactly what buyers receive. Include file formats, dimensions, and software compatibility. Moreover, preview images showing actual product contents reduce refund requests significantly. Checkout optimization matters enormously for conversion rates. Enable Shop Pay for one-click purchasing. Additionally, offer guest checkout to reduce friction for first-time buyers. Psychological pricing applies to digital products, too. $27 converts better than $25 despite a minimal difference. Similarly, $97 feels significantly cheaper than $100 psychologically. Bundle pricing increases average order value effectively. Offer individual templates at $15 and bundles at $39. Consequently, most customers choose the bundle for perceived better value. Test pricing regularly to find your optimal point. Furthermore, seasonal sales and limited-time offers create urgency without devaluing your products permanently. Pinterest drives enormous traffic for visual products. Create pins showing your templates in use. Moreover, optimizing pin titles and descriptions for search improves long-term visibility. Instagram works exceptionally well for showcasing digital planners and templates. Use Reels demonstrating your products solving real problems. Additionally, user-generated content from satisfied customers provides social proof. Email marketing remains the highest-ROI channel for digital products. Build your list through free downloadables or discount codes. Then, launch new products to your existing audience first. SEO matters more than many designers realize. Optimize product titles and descriptions for specific search terms. For instance, “Notion Template for Interior Designers” targets better than “Productivity Template.” Create comprehensive FAQs addressing common questions about file formats and usage. This reduces support tickets dramatically. Moreover, video tutorials showing product setup improve customer satisfaction significantly. Respond to inquiries within 24 hours to maintain positive reviews. Furthermore, going above expectations occasionally creates brand advocates who promote your products organically. Refund policies for digital products require careful consideration. Most designers offer 7-day satisfaction guarantees. However, clearly state that refunds are for product quality issues, not compatibility mistakes. The most profitable Shopify products for designers include Notion templates, Canva brand kits, UI/UX design systems, digital art for Frame TVs, and 3D printing files. These digital products offer 95-100% profit margins with zero inventory costs. Moreover, they scale infinitely without quality degradation. Designer earnings on Shopify range from $500 to $50,000+ monthly, depending on product type and marketing efforts. Digital products generate higher margins than physical goods. Furthermore, successful designers typically operate multiple income streams simultaneously. You don’t need coding skills to run a Shopify store successfully. The platform handles technical aspects automatically. However, understanding basic HTML/CSS helps with minor customization. Moreover, apps extend functionality without requiring development knowledge. Shopify gives you complete control over branding, pricing, and customer relationships. Conversely, marketplaces like Etsy charge listing fees and commissions while limiting customization. Additionally, you own customer data on Shopify, enabling email marketing and repeat sales. Price digital products based on the value and time savings they provide. Templates should cost $15-$50, comprehensive kits $75-$150, and educational courses $200-$500. Furthermore, bundle pricing increases average order value while providing customer savings. Yes, you can sell on Shopify while also listing on Creative Market or Gumroad. However, maintain consistent pricing across platforms. Moreover, offering exclusive products or bundles on your Shopify store incentivizes direct purchases. PDF works universally for planners and printables. Figma files suit UI/UX kits, while STL/OBJ formats are essential for 3D printing. Additionally, provide multiple formats when possible to accommodate different software preferences. Most designers see their first sales within 2-4 weeks of launching. However, building consistent monthly revenue requires 3-6 months of product development and marketing. Moreover, success accelerates once you establish a loyal customer base. Digital products offer superior profit margins and scalability. They require no inventory investment or shipping logistics. However, print-on-demand physical goods can complement digital offerings effectively. Therefore, many successful designers sell both strategically. Watermark preview images prominently to prevent unauthorized sharing. Additionally, use Shopify’s Digital Downloads app to limit download attempts. However, accept that some piracy is inevitable. Moreover, excellent customer service and updates encourage legitimate purchases over stolen files. Pinterest and Instagram drive the highest-quality traffic for visual products. Email marketing generates the best ROI for launches and promotions. Furthermore, SEO optimization creates passive traffic over time. Consequently, successful designers use all three channels strategically. Requirements vary by location, but most jurisdictions require business registration for consistent sales. Additionally, you’ll need to collect sales tax in applicable regions. Therefore, consult a local accountant about compliance requirements early. The Shopify products for designers market has matured beyond generic offerings. Success now requires strategic positioning, specific niche targeting, and multi-product revenue stacks. Moreover, the creators winning in 2026 treat their Shopify stores as sophisticated businesses rather than side projects. Your advantage as a designer lies in solving other creators’ problems. Subsequently, focus on products that eliminate friction in workflows or enhance professional capabilities. The demand for high-quality design tools continues to grow as the creator economy expands. Start with one product category that matches your skills perfectly. Then, expand strategically into complementary offerings over time. Most importantly, prioritize quality and customer experience above rapid product launches. Therefore, build a reputation that attracts customers through word-of-mouth and genuine value delivery. The designers earning $10K-$50K monthly through Shopify followed this blueprint systematically. They chose high-margin digital products, targeted specific niches, and marketed consistently across multiple channels. Title: Vivo V70: Check Pro ZEISS camera, expected price, launch date in India and full specs
URL: https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/news/new-updates/vivo-v70-check-pro-zeiss-camera-expected-price-launch-date-in-india-and-full-specs/articleshow/128107302.cms

With a 50MP ZEISS Night Telephoto camera and Snapdragon 8s Gen 3 performance, the V70 Elite offers flagship features at a competitive price point. Title: France's Vinci eyes India return with $2 billion Macquarie deal
URL: https://www.livemint.com/companies/vinci-macquarie-india-road-assets-acquisition-french-giant-return-11770619468473.html
Time Published: 2026-02-09T09:22:36Z
Full Content:
This is a Mint Premium article gifted to you. Subscribe to enjoy similar stories. MUMBAI : French concessions and construction company Vinci SA is nearing a $2 billion deal for Indian road assets owned by Australian asset manager Macquarie Group, paving the way for its return to the country after 11 years, two people aware of the matter told Mint. Macquarie launched the sale of its highway assets housed under Safeway Concessions Pvt. in September 2025, seeking around $1.2 billion for the portfolio, which it acquired in India's first toll-operate-transfer (ToT) auction in 2018 for ₹9,681 crore, or around $1.49 billion. The race for the assets narrowed to three contenders over the subsequent months: Vinci's concessions unit, Vinci Highways; IPO-bound EAAA India Alternatives Ltd's Sekura Roads; and KKR's Vertis Infrastructure Trust, as reported by The Economic Times in November 2025. Vinci has emerged as the frontrunner for the deal that could fetch $2 billion for the assets of Safeway Concessions, one of the two persons cited above said, on the condition of anonymity. The second person confirmed the development, adding that the deal may close in the coming weeks if both parties agree on certain terms. The deal, once completed, is likely to see the transfer of nine toll road projects spanning 681 kilometres in Andhra Pradesh and Gujarat to the French firm. The roads portfolio serves 38 million commuters each year and has over 1,700 employees. For Vinci, this would mark the culmination of its attempts to return to India, after it exited the country in 2015 amid policy shifts in the road assets industry. The company had originally sought to form a joint venture with BlackRock-backed infrastructure fund Global Infrastructure Partners (GIP), but talks fell through, the first person said. Vinci is likely to set up an India unit to house the road assets and avoid putting them into an infrastructure investment trust due to complex regulatory procedures, the second person added. Mint's emailed queries to Vinci and Macquarie for comments remained unanswered. Vinci Highways saw its annual revenue for the year ended December 2025 rise 11% on a like-for-like basis to €543 million. Its earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (Ebitda) came up to €282 million, or 51.9% of revenue. The company also registered free cash flows of close to €90 million. Investor interestVinci's interest in India's road assets is not an outlier. Media reports suggest Macquarie is also seeking a stake in the Canadian institutional investor CDPQ-backed Maple Infrastructure Trust. KKR has been consolidating its road portfolio under Vertis, as it aims to build a platform large enough to rival Singapore-based Cube Highways. Cube, meanwhile, acquired two annuity road assets in Jammu & Kashmir in 2025. Earlier in 2024, KKR-backed Highways Infrastructure Trust also signed definitive agreements to acquire a dozen road projects from PNC Infratech Ltd for ₹9,005 crore. The interest in roads is driven by factors such as the availability of a large number of assets that allow large institutional investors, including pension and sovereign wealth funds, to flock in. Macquarie's assets are also sweetened by the fact that they have a 30-year concession period. Also Read | India’s data centre boom turns to IPOs as AI-driven capex surges Vinci's India bet also comes at a time when India's road monetization has a steady outlook. Ratings agency Icra expects the sector to remain healthy in 2025-26. "The National Highways Authority of India (NHAI) has identified 24 assets across 12 states for monetization via ToT and infrastructure investment trusts (InvITs) modes. The identified 24 assets may garner ₹21,000-24,000 crore for the NHAI," the agency said in a March 2025 report. Icra also expects toll rate growth of around 2.5-3.9% in 2025-26, which, coupled with 3-5% traffic growth, is estimated to lead to 7-9% growth in toll collections. "Moreover, the competitive intensity is expected to remain high, as developers will continue to bid aggressively to shore up their order books," it noted. Title: How Jeff Bezos ruined the Washington Post and why he should sell it
URL: https://www.foxnews.com/opinion/how-jeff-bezos-ruined-washington-post-why-he-should-sell
Time Published: 2026-02-09T08:00:53Z
Full Content:
Former Washington Post face checker Glenn Kessler spoke with Fox News Digital about the paper's "absentee owner" Jeff Bezos and the challenges the news outlet faces. The first time I spoke to Jeff Bezos, he had founded Amazon as an online bookstore and made himself available to all kinds of journalists — a "political genius," said The New York Times Magazine, a "brilliant, charming, hyper, and misleadingly goofy mastermind." In 1999, having blown past the naysayers who scoffed at the strange notion of online retailing, the 35-year-old businessman was named Time’s Person of the Year. Nearly a decade-and-a-half later, as one of the world’s richest men, Bezos spent $250 million of his personal fortune to buy the Washington Post from Katharine Graham’s family. And now he should fold his cards and sell it. It’s a different era for the industry and a very different Bezos, one who is comfortable slashing a third of the paper’s staff. EX-WASHINGTON POST FACT CHECKER HITS ‘ABSENTEE OWNER’ BEZOS, TELLS HIM TO COMMIT TO SAVING PAPER OR SELL IT Having initially declared that "the duty of the paper is to the readers, not the owners," Bezos, whose Blue Origin company has federal contracts, is actively trying to repair his once-strained relationship with President Donald Trump. Amazon donated $1 million to Trump’s inauguration. While management has made more than its share of mistakes, I don’t think it’s an exaggeration to say that Bezos has destroyed what was once one of America’s great newspapers. I bring my personal history to the table. I spent 29 years at the Post, working for Bob Woodward’s investigative SWAT team, as Justice Department reporter, as New York bureau chief, and eventually as media reporter and columnist. In the 1980s and ’90s, when newspapers really mattered, the Post, while lacking the resources of The New York Times, delivered scoops with an all-star team, from politics (David Broder and Dan Balz) to sports (Tony Kornheiser, Michael Wilbon and Tom Boswell) to the metro desk (Woodward and Bernstein). And there was the freewheeling Style section of Sally Quinn and many other narrative writers. Jeff Bezos, founder and executive chairman of Amazon and owner of The Washington Post, looks out into the crowd during The New York Times annual DealBook summit at Jazz at Lincoln Center on Dec. 4, 2024, in New York City. (Michael M. Santiago/Getty Images) This was the paper of Watergate, helping to drive Richard Nixon from office, after defying his administration in running the Pentagon Papers, documenting the lies of the Vietnam War. It was the newspaper of the legendary Ben Bradlee, whose retirement I covered after being secretly briefed. Despite occasional blunders (such as Janet Cooke’s fraud), it was glamorized in two movies (Robert Redford and Dustin Hoffman in "All the President’s Men," Meryl Streep and Tom Hanks in "The Post"), inspiring legions of young graduates to go into journalism. When Bezos fired 300 journalists the other day, he completed the wave of destruction that had already left the Post a shell of its former self. Those dismissed included such remaining stars as Lizzie Johnson, who said she was "devastated" as she reported from the Ukraine war zone without heat or running water. And Marty Weil, a sardonic night-shift guy who has been at the paper for 60 years. And Sarah Ellison, an elegant writer who came from Vanity Fair. And this wrecking ball followed several earlier rounds of layoffs. Bezos doesn’t care. I just think he’s bored with the property he once believed would bring him instant credibility. He’s more interested in his rocket company. The Post is a blip on his global radar. PROMINENT PITTSBURGH NEWSPAPER THAT PREVIOUSLY ENDORSED TRUMP TO SHUTTER THIS YEAR I’m not in the camp that says Bezos should subsidize the paper forever just because he’s uber-rich. With the paper losing $100 million last year, he’s entitled to look for a path to profitability. But Bezos is getting absolutely hammered by the media. "We’re witnessing a murder," wrote Ashley Parker, now with the Atlantic. Liberal commentator Charlie Sykes offered this headline: "Gutless Billionaire Guts the Post." Former executive editor Marty Baron, who previously ran the prize-winning Boston Globe, declared: "Bezos’ sickening efforts to curry favor with President Trump have left an especially ugly stain of their own. This is a case study in near-instant, self-inflicted brand destruction." The Washington Post's building in D.C. The Post eliminated more than 300 jobs, laying off approximately 30% of its workforce. (Andrew Harnik/Getty Images) Onetime Metro editor David Maraniss, a mentor to so many at the paper, said: "He bought the Post thinking that it would give him some gravitas and grace that he couldn’t get from just billions of dollars, and then the world changed. Now I don’t think he gives a flying f---." In fairness, many newspapers have struggled with the collapse of their business model, as classifieds and advertising migrated online, and people could get breaking news from their phones or watches. Some converted to websites; the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette is closing in May. More than a quarter of American newspapers have folded in the past two decades. Back in 1981, the Washington Star, where I worked, was shuttered as afternoon papers became obsolete. But the Post is a classic case study of failure to adapt to the digital age. Katharine Graham was skeptical when she summoned me to explain this emerging universe. EX-WASHINGTON POST CHIEF BLASTS 'GUTLESS' BEZOS AS PAPER ROCKED BY MAJOR LAYOFFS In the Bezos era, the crashing waves of cutbacks meant asking readers to keep paying for a product that grew increasingly diminished over time, with its star players defecting to other major outlets. At first, Bezos took a hands-off approach, seemingly in sync with the newsroom culture. During Trump’s first term, he coined the slogan "Democracy Dies in Darkness." But there was a drastic shift in 2024. When the editorial board drafted an endorsement of Kamala Harris, Bezos killed it, which, as the owner, he has every right to do. Had he decided on a non-endorsement earlier, few would have cared. But Bezos wielded the ax a week before the election, and the furor was deafening. As the Post itself reported, more than 250,000 people canceled their subscriptions. Four months later, Bezos decreed that the editorial pages would focus every day on promoting "personal liberties" and "free markets," banning any attempt to offer opposing views. Opinion Editor David Shipley, whose section had won two Pulitzers, resigned, and other editors and columnists cut ties with the Post. President Donald Trump arrives at a dedication ceremony for a portion of Southern Boulevard that the Town of Palm Beach Council recently voted to rename President Donald J. Trump Boulevard at his Mar-a-Lago Club in Palm Beach, Florida, Jan. 16, 2026. (Julia Demaree Nikhinson/AP Photo) Meanwhile, the mogul socialized with the Trumps at Mar-a-Lago, and sat behind the president at his second inauguration. Bezos himself, as everyone knows, is now quite the jet-setter. He found himself in the middle of a tabloid scandal when the National Enquirer published lewd texts between Bezos and his girlfriend, Lauren Sanchez, as news of his divorce was breaking. The Enquirer also published pictures of his genitals, which he slammed as an attempt at blackmail. Bezos proposed to Sanchez on his 417-foot yacht, and they were married last spring in Venice, an extravaganza attended by the likes of Oprah Winfrey, Leonardo DiCaprio, Gayle King, Tom Brady and Kim Kardashian. The price tag for the multiday celebrations was somewhere between $20 million and $50 million. For Bezos, this was basically spare change. Peter Baker, a Post alumnus who is now chief White House correspondent at the Times and an MS NOW analyst, reports that Bezos’ net worth is up $224 billion since buying the Washington paper. So why does Bezos need the headache? He should unload this distressed asset to someone who would have a fresh shot at resuscitating The Washington Post from its near-death experience–though in all candor, it’s probably too late. A day after abolishing the Post’s sports section, CEO Will Lewis – who blew off the staff call explaining the layoffs – was walking the red carpet at the NFL Honors in San Francisco, an event leading up to the Super Bowl. Those who had lost their jobs, and their colleagues, were furious. CLICK HERE TO GET THE FOX NEWS APP Even worse, he wouldn’t allow the Post to write about the sweeping layoffs. Seriously. His terse farewell note thanked only Bezos. Back in the day, there would have been a half-dozen stories in the Post about the journalistic earthquake in its midst. But that was a long time ago. Howard Kurtz is a media and political analyst and the former host of FOX News Channel's MediaBuzz. Based in Washington, D.C., he joined the network in 2013 and regularly appears on Special Report with Bret Baier and The Story with Martha MacCallum among other programs.
